coredata.dc:title: MUA-modified Au nanocluster-driven fluorescence sensor for chromatographic test strips-based visual detection of patulin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Hongcheng
Xie, Zhenzhen
Xu, Shihao
Jiang, Changlong
coredata.dc:description: The relationship between human health and patulin (PAT) in the diet is a complex and intertwined one. The development of a sensing approach for the field detection of patulin is crucial, as the current approach lacks real-time detection capabilities and is costly in terms of material and technology. This paper presents a portable ratiometric fluorescence sensor that can be used to rapidly, accurately, and efficiently detect patulin in food items at the point of origin. The sensor employs a combination of sulfhydryl functionalized gold nanoclusters (MUA-AuNCs) and blue emission carbon dots (B-CDs), which have been engineered to serve as highly effective “on-off” nanoprobes. The modified sulfhydryl (SH) groups present on the gold clusters serve as specific recognition sites for patulin binding. The probes exhibit a discernible shift in hue, from orange-red to blue. The sensitivity detection limit (LOD) for patulin was found to be 0.019 μM, with a substantial linear correlation observed in the range of 0–2.2 μM. The objective of the combined chromatographic test strip and color recognition platform was to facilitate the sensitive, accurate, and real-time detection of patulin in foodstuffs, which is of paramount importance for the prevention of early disease. To facilitate rapid and straightforward preliminary testing of food security, it is anticipated that the integrated chromatographic strip ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform will be developed into portable home detection equipment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MUA-modified
Au nanocluster
Fluorescence visualization detection
Chromatographic test strip
Patulin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of blue emission carbon dots (B-CDs)
Synthesis of MUA functionalized gold nanoclusters (MUA-AuNCs)
Preparation of chromatographic test paper for detecting PAT
Detection of PAT in real samples
Probe characterization and sensing mechanisms for detection of PAT
Construction and optimization of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
Fluorescence chromatographic test strip application for detection of PAT and real sample analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) AuNCs, (B) MUA-AuNCs, and (C) MUA-AuNCs + PAT. The insets of A, B and C show the lattice spacing of AuNCs, MUA − AuNCs and MUA − AuNCs + PAT; The insets of A and B show the lattice spacing of AuNCs (D) UV–vis spectrum of PAT, CDs, HAuCl4, GSH-AuNCs, MUA − AuNCs, MUA − AuNCs + PAT and PAT; (E) The FT-IR spectra of CDs, GSH-AuNCs, MUA − AuNCs and MUA − AuNCs + PAT; (F) Zeta potential of GSH-AuNCs, MUA − AuNCs and MUA − AuNCs + PAT.

(A) Fluorescence lifetimes of MUA-AuNCs; (B) Fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs; (C) Fluorescence lifetimes of ratiometric fluorescent probe; (D) Fluorescence lifetimes of MUA-AuNCs + PAT; (E) Fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs + PAT; (F) The fluorescence lifetime of the ratiometric fluorescence probe after adding PAT.

(A) The fluorescence spectrum of PAT added to monochromatic MUA-AuNCs, and (B) the relationship between F/F0 ratio and PAT concentration; (C) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric fluorescent probe with the addition of PAT; The fluorescence intensity ratio was adjusted to a ratio of 1:4; The inset displays the corresponding fluorescent photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp and the CIE diagram of fluorescent under the adding of PAT. (D) The linear relationship between the ratio of I620 /I450 and the concentration of PAT.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of ratiometric fluorescent probes with the addition of mycotoxins, ions, and organics (the insets show the corresponding fluorescence photographic images under a 365 nm UV lamp); (B) Fluorescent responses to various mycotoxin, ions, and organics; The selectivity tests were done in purified water with the addition of 2.2 μM of various interfering substances.

(A) Schematic illustration of the chromatographic test strip. The color change of the fluorescence of the strip after adding PAT was measured by a fluorescence analyzer; (B) Fluorescence color change of chromatographic test paper when PAT content in apple juice, tomato juice, wheat, and peanut juice is 0–2.2 μM.
The sensing mechanism of ratiometric fluorescent probes for visual detection of PAT.
The results for the determination of patulin in apple juice, tomato juice, wort juice, and peanut juice were compared by chromatographic strip and liquid chromatograph.
Comparison of patulin detection performance based on different sensors.

row.entry: Apple and tomato juice, peanut and wheat

head.ce:title: MUA-modified Au nanocluster-driven fluorescence sensor for chromatographic test strips-based visual detection of patulin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, Hefei 230069, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, Hefei 230069, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei 230031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photovoltaic and Energy Conservation Materials, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei 230031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photovoltaic and Energy Conservation Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Photovoltaic and Energy Conservation Materials, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei 230031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The relationship between human health and patulin (PAT) in the diet is a complex and intertwined one. The development of a sensing approach for the field detection of patulin is crucial, as the current approach lacks real-time detection capabilities and is costly in terms of material and technology. This paper presents a portable ratiometric fluorescence sensor that can be used to rapidly, accurately, and efficiently detect patulin in food items at the point of origin. The sensor employs a combination of sulfhydryl functionalized gold nanoclusters (MUA-AuNCs) and blue emission carbon dots (B-CDs), which have been engineered to serve as highly effective “on-off” nanoprobes. The modified sulfhydryl (SH) groups present on the gold clusters serve as specific recognition sites for patulin binding. The probes exhibit a discernible shift in hue, from orange-red to blue. The sensitivity detection limit (LOD) for patulin was found to be 0.019 μM, with a substantial linear correlation observed in the range of 0–2.2 μM. The objective of the combined chromatographic test strip and color recognition platform was to facilitate the sensitive, accurate, and real-time detection of patulin in foodstuffs, which is of paramount importance for the prevention of early disease. To facilitate rapid and straightforward preliminary testing of food security, it is anticipated that the integrated chromatographic strip ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform will be developed into portable home detection equipment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety and a healthy lifestyle are strongly intertwined. Food tainted with mycotoxins can be detrimental to people's health. Patulin (PAT) is an exceptionally poisonous mycotoxin that is heat-stable, water-soluble, and commonly present in rotting fruits, juices, and crops [1,2]. Consuming food tainted with PAT could result in toxicity issues that could be immediate or chronic, in addition to possible long-term health hazards [3–5]. The development of a simple, immediate, and sensitive detection technique is required due to the high toxicity and ubiquitous prevalence of patulin in food.

One practical and commonly employed approach to detecting mycotoxin is immunoassay. With the help of a FAM-Apt/Cr(OH)3 aptamer sensor, Li et al. effectively identified PAT [6]. Some useful applications, particularly portable fluorescence sensing devices, are still limited in the foreseeable future due to the challenges associated with collecting antigens and antibodies. Fluorescence analysis has garnered significant attention owing to its exceptional sensitivity, selectivity, and versatility, owing to the emergence of carbon dots (CDs), quantum dots (QDs), gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), and other fluorescent materials [7–9]. Furthermore, surface chemical engineering of different luminous materials has demonstrated significant potential uses. Liu et al. created an “on” sensor for the detection of PAT by synthesizing sulfidation-functionalized nitrogen-doped blue emission carbon dots (B-CDs) via the amide reaction of B-CDs with L-cysteine [10]. A small molecule fluorescent probe called FITC-Lys was created by Li et al. to enable visual fluorescence detection of PAT [11]. This probe works by reacting with the Lys group to produce fluorescein dimer. While these techniques increase the accuracy and rapidity of PAT detection, the monochromatic fluorescence sensing method which modifies a single fluorescence intensity is unreliable because to its susceptibility to environmental and probe concentration fluctuations. The human eye is significantly more sensitive to color changes than it is to brightness changes, consequently the ratiometric fluorescence sensing approach eliminates the aforementioned restrictions and enhances detection accuracy.

With its numerous material properties and changeable electronic structure, gold is one of the most promising and researched materials at the nanoscale [12–15]. Precious metal nanoclusters have garnered an enormous amount of attention lately owing to their distinctive molecular-like characteristics [16–18]. Of these instances, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), which are composed of several Au atoms, are particularly intriguing. It was possible to successfully prepare quantum-sized fluorescent AuNCs (<2 nm) with different ligands or templates (for instance, OH-terminated polyamino-dendrimer [19], bovine serum albumin [20], glutathione [21]) by top-down etching of preformed AuNPs (>2 nm) and bottom-up reduction of metal ions. This advancement has received an abundance of inspiration and encouragement. Although it is still in its early stages, surface chemical engineering of luminous AuNCs presents significant obstacles [22]. Their superior sensitivity to surface ligands and the outside environment, associated with their larger surface-area-to-volume ratio, are the primary causes [23]. Consequently, rather than employing particular functionalized ligands for haphazard and uneven ligand modifications, direct synthesis is required for the production of functionalized luminous AuNCs.

In this study, we have devised a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor that is both simple and rapid to synthesize. Subsequent hydrothermal reduction, the gold clusters were surface-engineered into generating sulfhydryl functionalized MUA-AuNCs with a vibrant orange-red fluorescence emission. For the purpose of to develop a ratiometric fluorescence probe, blue B-CDs had been added to MUA-AuNCs as an internal reference. The sulfhydryl-functionalized MUA-AuNCs exhibit excellent stability and identification ability with regard to patulin. Further investigation in an alkaline environment with detection limits as low as 0.019 μM may prove to be a fruitful avenue of research. The MUA-AuNCs orange-red fluorescence was turned off and the B-CDs fluorescence was turned on for the purpose of selectively detecting patulin visually considering the powerful binding affinity of patulin molecules to MUA-AuNCs surface ligands. Likewise, a chromatographic strip with continuous fluorescence colorimetric mode has been constructed on cellulose membrane sprayed by a synovometer using this extraordinarily sensitive and selective fluorescence response. This effectively eliminates the uncertainty of immunoassay and enables real-time detection of patulin in aqueous solution or actual grain samples. The probe's potential as an invaluable instrument for tracking patulin in food has been demonstrated, and as consequently, it can be widely used in food safety supervision and mycotoxin detection.

Patulin (PAT), Aflatoxin A (AFB1), Ochratoxin (OTA), deoxynivalenol (DON), fumonitoxin B1 (FB1), zearalenone (ZEN), mucor (TH), citrinin (CIT), reduced glutathione (GSH), undecylmercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA), gold tetrachlorate trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) Medicines and equipment such as dialysis box (1000 Da) were supplied by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were not further purified unless otherwise noted. The experimental water is all ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) produced by the Millipore water purification system. Apple juice, tomato juice, wort, and peanuts come from the local market.

A UV–vis spectrometer was used to assess UV–visible absorption spectra (Agilent Cary 3500). Agilent Cary Eclipse spectrometer was used to measure fluorescence spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were gathered using a high-performance electron spectrometer manufactured by Thermo Scientific, the ESCALAB 250. An Agilent FT-IR spectrometer has been used to analyze infrared spectra. AGL-9406 portable UV light was used to capture fluorescence with a Canon EOS 600D digital camera (365 nm). All fluorescent photos were taken in the 365 nm range using a Canon EOS600D digital camera under a UVC portable UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of blue emission carbon dots (B-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The B-CDs were created and only altered, following the procedure described by Liu et al [24]. After dissolving 1.0 g of sodium citrate and 0.52 g of polyacrylamide in 20 mL of distilled water and thoroughly mixing the mixture, it was put into a 50 mL reactor coated with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated to 200 °C for three hours. Following the reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature, and the solution containing blue carbon dots was dried in an oven at 60°C after being filtered by dialysis (1000 Da) for four hours. The blue carbon dots powder was lastly diluted in distilled water (1 mg/mL) and stored at 4°C in the refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MUA functionalized gold nanoclusters (MUA-AuNCs)
ce:section.ce:para: Following is a description of a typical synthesis procedure. After thoroughly dissolving the GSH (92.2 mg) in 14.9 mL of water, the HAuCl4 liquid (5.1 mL, 1 %), was rapidly added. The fully combined solution is subsequently allowed to react for half an hour at room temperature to create the AuNCs-GSH complex. During this time, the light brown solutions gradually become pale yellow before it turns nearly colorless. The AuNCs aqueous solution (4 mL) was added to the solution made by mixing 15.7 mL of water with 13.1 mg of MUA and 0.3 mL of 1 M NaOH. According to UV light, the combination appears fluorescent orange-red for a brief period of time. After that, it is at room temperature for five hours without stirring, during which the solution doesn't change color.

For the production of a ratiometric probe with a fluorescence intensity ratio of 1:4, 10 μL B-CDs and 60 μL MUA-AuNCs were well mixed in a quartz test tube in a 3.5 mL quartz cuphore and diluted to 2 mL with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4). After adding 10 µL of PAT solution each time, the solution's color changed under an ultraviolet lamp, and the fluorescence spectrum of the ratiometric fluorescence probe at 340 nm single wavelength was recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The same setup and three repetitions were used for all of the selectivity and anti-interference tests. The solution's fluorescent images were captured using UV light with an absorption wavelength of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of chromatographic test paper for detecting PAT
ce:section.ce:para: Afterward, 0.3 mL of B-CDs and 3 mL of MUA-AuNCs were produced, and the ratiometric probe solution was placed in the gold spray pump. The device uses an imported step pump with 24,000 steps to drive a multi-color fluorescent paper sensor. The liquid output accuracy is controlled at 0.01 μL. The step pump additionally pushes out the probe solution when it is sprayed, repeating the process 50 times. The quantitative spray point on the fiber film is marked at a distance of 6 mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PAT in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the strategy's sustainability, the fluorescence sensor constructed for the present investigation was used to detect patulin in apple juice, tomato juice, wheat, and peanuts. The local market provided the wheat, peanuts, apple juice, and tomato juice used to conduct the experiment. Previous to the wheat and peanut being transformed into solution, the samples were pretreated using the apple juice, tomato juice, wort, and peanut juice pretreatment techniques described by Wu et al [25]. First, 1 mL of the juice sample and 1 mL of ethyl acetate were transferred to a 5 mL centrifuge tube. The resulting mixture was then fluctuated and centrifuged (1665g, 5 min). The supernatant is extracted and dried utilizing a nitrogen purge following centrifugation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probe characterization and sensing mechanisms for detection of PAT
ce:section.ce:para: AuNCs were produced by hydrothermally reducing the Au source using GSH and a strong emission fluorescence probe, while MUA-AUNCs were produced by altering the surface of 1-Mercaptoundecanoic acid (MUA). Scheme 1 describes the formation and reaction process of MUA-AuNCs. The reaction between HAuCl4 and GSH is the initial step. Under 365 mm of UV light, the resultant combination is nearly colorless and does not glow. Au3+ is reduced to Au+ by GSH, and then Au+ is promptly coordinated with the sulfhydryl group to form the GSH-AuNCs complex. It was utilized as a reducing agent to lower GSH-Au, construct dual-ligand co-functionalized fluorescent AuNCs that fluoresced orange-red when exposed to 365 mm of UV light and create the ratiometric probe system employing B-CDs. The −SH on the surface of AuNCs interacted with PAT molecules after PAT had been incorporated, facilitating energy transfer between the two separate molecules and obscuring the fluorescence. The probe solution progressively changed coloration from orange-red fluorescence under 365 nm UV light to blue, and the entire process was visible to the naked eye [22,26,27]. The synthetically produced MUA-AuNCs present an emission spectrum with a center at λ = 610 nm while excited at λ = 320 nm (Fig. S1). When left at room temperature for two days, the fluorescence of the produced solution remains almost intact, demonstrating good fluorescence stability (Fig. S2).

With an average diameter of approximately 1.32 nm, TEM was implemented to characterize the exterior morphology and particle size of AuNCs, as shown in Fig. 1 A. AuNCs have a uniform distribution and have a spherical shape that is fundamentally monodisperse. As illustrated in Fig. 1B, quantum dots exhibit modest agglomeration and a slight increase in diameter with modification of MUA, owing to covalent coordination on the surface of AuNCs, while maintaining good monodispersed characteristics. The (1 1 1) lattice plane of AuNCs is assigned a lattice fringe spacing distance of approximately 0.26 nm, as demonstrated by the HRTEM image of Fig. 1B. The aforementioned experimental findings show that the MUA-AuNCs nanoprobe, which has good chemical stability and maintains a stable molecular state, has been manufactured satisfactorily. Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. 1C, organic molecules chelate on the surface of AuNCs through covalent bonding when patulin is added, thereby increasing the particle size. Furthermore, the DLS results in Fig. S4A provide more precise particle size data, which depicts an average diameter of 1.68 nm for MUA-AuNCs. On the other hand, the DLS data revealed that the size of the MUA-AuNCs + PAT measured approximately 2.18 nm from Fig. S4B when patulin was added to the MUA-AuNCs, which was compatible with the TEM results.

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy of single B-CDs and MUA-AuNCs were analyzed to further investigate their optical characteristics. The two distinctive absorption peaks of B-CDs, located at 246 nm and 347 nm, are ascribed to the creation of the surface state excitation deficiencies and the π–π* transitions of the CC bond [28]. Energy transfer is achievable given that MUA-AuNCs exhibit a broad UV absorption peak in the range of 225 nm to 275 nm, whereas PAT exhibits an enormous absorption peak at 225 nm that overlaps and exceeds than that of MUA-AuNCs. The occurrence of energy transfer between MUA-AuNCs and PAT is further demonstrated by Fig. 1D, which reveals that when PAT is introduced to MUA-AuNCs, the UV absorption peak intensity in the 225–275 nm range is dramatically decreased.

The FT-IR spectra of GSH-AuNCs, MUA-AuNCs, and B-CDs were measured to confirm the successful synthesis of MUA-AuNCs. The FT-IR spectrum of the initial GSH-AuNCs, as shown in Fig. 1E, demonstrated that the characteristic absorption peak of sulfhydryl (SH) disappeared at 2940 cm−1, indicating that GSH-AuNCs were formed by AuS bond. However, after adding MUA, the characteristic absorption peak of SH appeared at 2920 cm−1 (Fig. 1E), indicating that the sulfhydryl modification was successful. Moreover, XPS can be employed to determine GSH-AuNCs, MUA-AuNCs, and GSH- MUA-AuNCs (Figs. S5, S6, and S7). Examining the MUA-AuNCs spectra (Fig. S6) reveals the presence of Au, S, C, N, and O elements, and the spectra of the Au 4f region can be separated into two distinct peaks that are attributed to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2, respectively (Figs. S6 and S7). The high-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s, N1s, and O1s of B-CDs were generated independently, and the total XPS spectra of B-CDs were measured (Fig. S8).

Additionally presented is the B-CDs fourier transformation in the infrared range (Fig. 1D). The NH/OH vibration that stretches is responsible for the characteristic absorption bands of the blue carbon dots at 3450 cm−1, whereas the asymmetrical deformation vibration of CO or the bending vibration of NH is linked to the characteristic peak at 1620 cm−1. The distinctive peak at 1100 cm−1 is linked to the stretching of CO vibration, whereas the absorbent peak at 1404 cm−1 corresponds to either OH stretching resonance or CN stretching vibration. The outermost layer of B-CDs may include a significant amount of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups, as demonstrated by infrared spectrum studies. A ratiometric probe was created through the combination of B-CDs and MUA-AuNCs at a fluorescent intensity that was 1:4 (I450/I620) (Fig. 3C). This produced an intense orange-red fluorescence and two emissions peaks at an identical excitation wavelength of 340 nm, which corresponded to a single B-CDs and MUA-AuNCs.

Further proof of the reaction mechanism is provided by the Zeta potential diagram in Fig. 1F. The surface of GSH is negatively charged due to the ligand containing carboxyl groups. The simple fact that every component other than Au3+ is negatively charged demonstrates that Au3+ is the medium used to transfer energy from GSH-AuNCs to MUA-AuNCs and transfer energy from MUA-AuNCs to PAT. The charge transfer process from MUA-AuNCs to PAT is additionally aided by the simultaneous decrease in the zeta potential of Au3+/MUA-AuNCs and GSH-AuNCs in that order.

To confirm this, experiments were conducted on the fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs, MUA-AuNCs, MUA-AuNCs + B-CDs, B-CDs + PAT, and MUA-AuNCs + B-CDs + PAT (Fig. 2 ). We discovered that following AuNCs surface modification with sulfhydryl (−SH), the fluorescence lifetime of orange-red emission did not change considerably. When PAT was introduced, the lifetime of the orange-red fluorescence was greatly decreased. Furthermore, we can observe that adding PAT to the ratiometric probe solution considerably lengthens the fluorescence lifespan of blue emission, supporting the possibility of electron transfer between PAT and MUA-AuNCs.

The measurement of the amount of patulin is predicated on the addition of PAT and the subsequent quenching of orange-red fluorescence. The blue fluorescence remains unaffected. The proportion of the probe's orange-red fluorescence could be suppressed by PAT, however the blue fluorescence stayed consistent. The aforementioned phenomenon can be described by the suppression of fluorescence caused by the electrostatic attraction of MUA-AuNCs induced by aggregation triggered by PAT. It is possible to use B-CDs and MUA-AuNCs proportion probes together using the fluorescence recovery approach. When 2.2 μM PAT was incorporated, the ratiometric fluorescence probe's initial orange-red fluorescence was extinguished. Yet since B-CDs did not react with PAT, its blue fluorescence gradually emerged, prompting the solution's fluorescence color to gradually transform from orange-red to blue. This mechanism can be seen in corresponding TEM pictures, where scattered MUA-AuNCs particles with an average size of roughly 2 nm are slightly consolidated following the addition of PAT. The addition of PAT had no significant impact on the fluorescence of individual B-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and optimization of the ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: We adjusted an assortment of experimental variables which included the excitation length of wavelength, reaction duration, pH concentration of the mixture containing the investigation solution, along with ratio of the probe fluorescence intensity, that might have had an impact on the effectiveness of the fluorescence detecting process.

Fluorescence AuNCs exhibit promise for applications in biological detection and marking processes. We first examined how pH affected the strength of the fluorescence and the resilience of these nanoclusters before testing with our MUA-AuNCs. In order to safeguard the stability of the solution system, we utilized PBS buffer as the solvent for examining the impact of the solution's pH on the probe's stability and fluorescence intensity. Yet, a solution of sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid is used to change the pH. Outstanding stability has been demonstrated by MUA-AuNCs, which only form precipitates from the solution in 5 mM PBS buffers at pH 2.0 or 3.0 but maintain persistence at pH 5.0 or above. As an alternative, we employ MUA as the single functionalized receptor to create other AuNCs (such as MUA-AuNCs). On the other hand, MUA-AuNCs frequently precipitate out of solutions around pH 2.0–5.0 and show unstable behavior in solutions with acids (Fig. S9) [29]. In the subsequent studies, a 5 mM buffering solution with a pH of 10 was chosen as the detection buffer simply because of its simple nature of use and the sensitivity of the patulin measurement (the ideal pH is approximately 10.0).

Subsequently, the proportion of the probe's fluorescence strength ratio was investigated. For the development of an appropriate probe for patulin detection, B-CDs, and MUA-AuNCs have been combined with the fluorescence intensity ratios of 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4 (I450/I620), sequentially (Fig. S10). The most favorable response to patulin was discovered in the ratiometric probe, which was created by combining B-CDs and A MUA-AuNCs at a 1:4 fluorescent intensity ratio (I450/I620). Next, the most appropriate wavelength for excitation was investigated. The B-CDs excitement peak has been identified at 226 nm and 348 nm, respectively (Fig. S11A). The B-CDs brightness of fluorescence developed initially and then dismissed peaking at 340 nm, while the excitation wavelength was changed between 320 nm and 360 nm. In a comparable vein, the fluorescence strength of the MUA-AuNCs peaks at 320 nm whenever the excitation wavelength switches from 340 nm and 380 nm. A 340 nm polychromatic fluorescence investigation has been selected as the perfect excitation wavelength to guarantee that the fluorescence of the two materials is activated simultaneously. By simultaneously excitation of the fluorescence of two materials in a mixture of fluorescent materials with a wavelength of 340 nm, a multicolor fluorescent probe can be created. Upon achieving the perfect response time, it is discovered that the ratiometric probe may respond appropriately to the material being detected at roughly 10 s. (Fig. S12A).

As previously mentioned, incubating the probe concentration for 5 min at a pH of 10 generated the best results. We may additionally obtain fluorescence detection findings immediately because of the incredibly rapid response time.

The sulfhydryl group (SH) on the surface of AuNCs functions as the target molecular binding site to chelate with PAT upon its addition [30]. While the blue fluorescence of B-CDs as an internal reference progressively emerges, representing a fluorescence transition from orange-red to blue, the orange-red fluorescence ceases to exist when PAT is present due to the interaction between AuNCs and PAT.

By presenting a combination probe made of B-CDs and MUA-AuNCs, a fluorescent “on-off” method was established for recording PAT. The quantitative detection of PAT was measured employing a combination of orange MUA-AuNCs and B-CDs at a fluorescence strength ratio of 1:4, where the orange MUA-AuNCs suppressed the orange-red fluorescence. The orange-red fluorescence at 620 nm gradually disappeared as the PAT concentration arose from 0 μM to 2.2 μM, but the blue fluorescence remaining and the hue changed from red to dark purple to blue (Fig. 3 C, video S1). Additionally, The CIE diagram inset in Fig. 3C further validated the significant variation in fluorescence chromaticity from red to blue. Moreover, there was an extremely strong linear association (R2 = 0.997 between the concentrations of I450/I620 and PAT concentration in the concentration range of 0–2.2 μM (Fig. 3D). A measurement of three times a background average deviation (3δ/slope) of the slope was employed to determine the limit of detection (LOD), which turned out to be 0.019 μM.

The remainder of optical identification of PAT in the fraction of the probed system utilises B-CDs as the reference fluorescence and MUA-AuNCs as the response unit. The performance of ratio composites probes and monochromatic fluorescent probes was assessed through investigations into the fluorescence response of monochromatic fluorescent probes to PAT. The fluorescence of MUA-AuNCs was quenched upon the addition of PAT, and the orange-red fluorescence progressively diminished (Fig. 3A). The monochromatic fluorescent probe's hue varied noticeably as the PAT concentration approached nearer to 2.8 μM. Within the range of 0–2.8 μM (R2 = 0.998), the fluorescence strength of MUA-AuNCs revealed a linear relationship with PAT concentrations, and the detection limit was 0.76 μM (Fig. 3B). However, uniform ratio color fluctuation changes are more feasible for the naked eye for observation given that human eyes are more sensitive to color changes than individual color chromaticity changes. In contrast, color changes can be recognized by adding 2.2 μM PAT when implementing a ratiometric probe for visual detection, assuming that human eyes are more sensitive to color changes than chroma changes. Nevertheless, the fluorescence response of the ratiometric fluorescence probe to PAT showed that the detection limit was 0.019 μM (Fig. 3C), and the fluorescence level of ratio showed a linear relationship with PAT concentration in the range of 0–2.2 μM (R2 = 0.997). As a result, the composite probe working in combination with B-CDs and MUA-AuNCs performs better and can identify PAT with higher accuracy and sensitivity.

Likewise, comparable tests were carried out to eliminate the impact of PAT fluorescence in the absence of MUA-AuNCs. It is further confirmed that the interaction involving PAT and customized MUA-AuNCs particles is the basis for the detecting concept behind PAT, as blue fluorescence remained constant even at 10 μM PAT addition. Consequently, the functionalized MUA-AuNCs system's ratiometric probe shows its sensitivity and specificity in tracking PAT.

Two important aspects in assessing a fluorescence probe's practical application value are its selectivity and anti-interference. Recording the fluorescence variations of ratiometric probes by adding several types of interfering chemicals to their solution is a crucial way to assess the selectivity of ratiometric probes for PAT detection [31]. Fig. 4 A illustrates the findings of a series of fluorescence intensity response experiments carried out under the same settings for various ions and pesticides that might generate interference. Purified water was used for the selectivity and interference experiments, fluorescent responses to various mycotoxins, the selectivity tests were done in purified water with the addition of 2.2 μM of mycotoxin into a fluorescent probe solution (Fig. S13). In the meantime, there will be plenty more interfering compounds, including metal cations and anions, as well as other small biological molecules, that will interfere with the probe's ability to detect actual samples. We proceed with investigating AuNCs' selectivity and anti-interference on their own. According to the fluorescence curve in Fig. S14, which is further supported by fluorescence photographs, various kinds of interfering chemical compounds do not have a quenching impact on the 620 nm fluorescence peak when 2.2 μL of them have been added to AuNCs solution. It is apparent the ratio probe's AuNCs exhibit strong selectivity and anti-interference, both of which support the precision and dependability of AuNCs in actual samples. Fluorescent responses to various mycotoxin, ions, and organics, the selectivity tests were done in purified water with the addition of 2.2 μM of various interfering substances. (The inset displays the matching fluorescent photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp) (Fig. 4A and Fig. S14). deoxynivalenol (DON), ochratoxin (OTA), aflatoxin A (AFB1), zearalenone (ZEN), mucor (TH), citrinin (CIT), ions of metals (K+, Mg2+, Fe2+), anions (Br−, F−, NO2 −), vitamin B(VB), vitamin C (VC), and lysine (Lys) to track the ways in which all of these compounds could interact with the sensor and alter the fluorescence the level of effort (Fig. 4A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence chromatographic test strip application for detection of PAT and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The stepping pump was employed to release 0.3 mL B-CDs and 3 mL MUA-AuNCs when the probe solution was injected. The effective preparation of portable non-immunochromatographic strips involved 50 repetitions of the operation, marking the quantitative spray spots on the fiber membrane at 6 mm intervals (Fig. 5 A). The chromatographic test strip was filled with the prepared real sample solution, the color shifted from orange-red to blue when exposed to a 365 mm UV lamp, indicating that the PAT detection in the real sample had occurred (Fig. 5).

As demonstrated in Table 1 , various amounts of patulin (0.1 μ M, 1.2 μ M, and 2.2 μ M) were incorporated into apple juice, tomato juice, wort, which is and peanut juice ((Fig. 5B and Fig. S15). The levels of recovery were subsequently determined. The compound patulin concentrations in the juice sample varied from 87.9 % to 101.2 %, with accompanying deviations from the mean ranging from 2.09 % to 3.46 % (Table 1).

We simulated the linear relationship between the addition of PAT and the value change by employing a smartphone to read the solution chroma change of the ratiometric fluorescence probe when a specific concentration of PAT was added, specifically the red value/blue value.

The fluorescent probe solution gradually changed from red to blue under UV light as the PAT concentration ascended from 0.1 µM to 2.2 µM. This could be utilized as an initial basis for PAT detection in smartphone applications. Via the utilization of a smartphone application (color recognizer), the fluorescent probe's color information (RGB values) can be gathered. The findings of reading the red and blue channel correlation values and figuring out their proportions reveal that there is an appropriate linear association between the two channels ratio and the PAT concentration (R2 = 0.993). Similar to the above, we employed a mobile phone to read the color change value of the fluorescence chromatography test strip after implementing various PAT concentrations. We then estimated the ratio of red to blue and found that there was an attractive a linear connection as well (R2 = 0.996).

Table 2 displays the identifying techniques utilized in this investigation along with additional techniques for patulin detection. The non-immunization fluorescent approach explored in this study has several advantages over typical detection immunochromatography techniques: it has a faster sensor reaction time, a lower identification limit, a better visual effect, less environmental impact, and a comparable target recognition recovery rate. This indicates even further that the suggested non-immunofluorescence sensor is appropriate for routine PAT identification applications since it offers a reliable way to detect PAT levels in real samples quickly and steadily.

To circumvent the restrictions of real sample detection and the instability of conventional immunoassay, we established a chromatographic test strip based on the fluorescence tackle that allowed portable visual detection of patulin. To gauge patulin (PAT), a ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe consisting of orange-red MUA-AuNCs (MUA molecular functionalization gold nanoclusters) and blue emission carbon dots (B-CDs) was manufactured using the fluorescence “on-off” methodology. While PAT exerts no effect on the blue fluorescence of B-CDs, the sulphoyl group (SH) on the surface of MUA-AuNCs binds specifically to PAT and energy transfer takes place, suppressing the orange and red fluorescence of AuNCs. As as a consequence, in the range of 0–2.2 μM, the ratiometric probe's fluorescence demonstrates a substantial linear correlation with PAT concentration with detection limits as low as 0.019 μM. More significantly, we employed an inkjet synoviometer for creating chromatographic strips based on ratiometric fluorescence probes. In furtherance of avoiding the negative aspects of the antigen–antibody method, we were additionally competent to visually identify the abundance of patulin residues in apple juice, tomato juice, wort, and peanut juice. In summary, the surface-functionalized gold nanoparticle-based fluorescent probe represents a sensitive and potent way to detect patulin. Likewise, patulin detection in food samples is additionally rendered practicable by the chromatographic test strip that was specifically built for this purpose. This affords strong technological advancements assurance for food safety and can potentially be utilized for the detection of other potentially hazardous substances.
The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program (2021YFD2000200, 2023YFD1702900), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos, 22476196, 21876175, U23A20265), and the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Provincial (2408085QB054).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125736.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
